title: Facilitators: An Emerging Profession
description: Understand what cyberdelic facilitators do, their training, ethical obligations, and how the profession is evolving.

sections.title: Who Facilitators Are: A Genuinely New Role
sections.content:
Cyberdelic facilitators exist in a professional space that has no exact predecessor. They are not therapists — they don't diagnose, treat, or work within clinical frameworks, and doing so without a clinical license would constitute practicing without one. They are not life coaches — the potential for genuine altered states creates a responsibility profile that coaching certifications don't address. They are not meditation teachers — the technology layer adds preparation obligations and technical competencies that traditional contemplative training doesn't cover.

What they are: trained guides who create the conditions for safe, intentional, supported cyberdelic experiences — managing the before, during, and after of a session with someone navigating non-ordinary states of consciousness through technology.

People coming to facilitation typically arrive from adjacent roles: meditation and mindfulness teachers adding VR to their practice, life coaches integrating consciousness technology, wellness practitioners expanding their service offerings, and some with clinical backgrounds who are choosing to work in a non-clinical capacity.

The profession is young. Standards are still forming. The window for early practitioners to shape what it becomes — ethically, professionally, structurally — is open right now.

sections.title: The Three-Phase Facilitation Role
sections.content:
Effective facilitation isn't just what happens during the VR session. It's what happens in all three phases:

BEFORE — Preparation and Holding

  • Assess readiness: Is this person in a suitable state of mental, emotional, and physical health?
  • Screen for contraindications: Active psychosis, severe dissociation history, photosensitive epilepsy, certain medications.
  • Set expectations: What this experience is, what it is not, what they might encounter.
  • Establish informed consent & trust: The relationship quality before the session significantly determines what becomes possible during it.

DURING — Presence and Support

  • Technical support: Ensuring hardware functions, managing transitions without breaking state.
  • Monitor state: Reading non-verbal cues, recognizing difficulty vs. depth.
  • Manage pacing & Hold container: Maintaining a calm, grounded presence that communicates safety without intrusion.

AFTER — Integration

  • Process the experience: Space for the person to articulate and make meaning.
  • Support integration: Practical guidance on carrying insights into daily life.
  • Follow-up & Know when to refer: Recognizing when material surfaces that requires clinical support beyond the facilitation scope.

media.aiPrompt: Three-phase facilitation visual — a sleek timeline or arc showing Before / During / After. Each phase has a minimalist icon (Preparation/Holding, Presence/Support, Integration) and subtle glowing connecting lines to indicate flow. Professional, modern aesthetic.

sections.title: Training: What Competent Facilitation Requires
sections.content:
Cyberdelic facilitation training programs (like this one) are emerging. The general competency framework looks like this:

  • Technical Skills (~20%): VR hardware setup, hygiene protocols, platform navigation, biofeedback integration, contraindication screening.
  • Facilitation Skills (~40%): Preparation interview technique, active presence, non-verbal communication, state recognition, difficult experience management.
  • Theoretical Foundation (~20%): Cyberdelic field overview, set and setting principles, altered states theory, ethics frameworks.
  • Supervised Practice (~20%): Observed facilitation sessions, peer co-facilitation, and case consultation.

Typical total hours: 40–100. No standardized national certification exists yet — professional standards are emerging organically through organizations, platform training tracks, and university-affiliated programs.

sections.title: Professional Standards and Ethics
sections.content:
The absence of formal regulation doesn't mean the absence of professional obligations. Several core principles have emerged as near-universal:

  • Informed Consent: Every participant must understand the experience, risks, facilitator role limitations, and that they can stop at any time. It's an ongoing conversation.
  • Confidentiality: What happens in a session stays in that container, with explicit exceptions for imminent safety risk.
  • Scope of Practice: Facilitators are not therapists. When clinical material surfaces (trauma activation, psychosis, suicidality), the ethical response is referral to clinical support. This boundary protects both client and facilitator.
  • Knowing When to Refer: The practitioners who consistently demonstrate minimum professional maturity refer readily and without ego — their role is to hold space for exploration, not manage clinical crises.
  • Dual Relationship Avoidance: Sexual, financial, or other power-compromising dynamics are ethically prohibited.

sections.title: Revenue, Economics, and Career Reality
sections.content:
Facilitation is a profession — which means it requires viable economics.

Session TypeTypical Price Range
Private individual (1–2 hrs)$75–$300
Group session (per person)$30–$100
Day retreat$300–$800
Multi-day retreat$500–$3,000+
Institutional contract (per day)$1,000–$5,000+

The sustainability realities: Building a practice takes 1–3 years. Hybrid models (combining facilitation with teaching or wellness work) are the norm. Institutional pathways offer more reliable revenue than purely private practice. Early practitioners are building the profession that later ones will inherit.

scenes.narrative:
Note: As you work through this scenario, pay attention to the professional reasoning, but also notice your own reaction. Does navigating this kind of boundary feel like work you're called to do?

You receive the following message from a potential new client:

"Hi — I found you through a friend who worked with you last year. I've been seeing a therapist for PTSD for 18 months and I think VR could really help me. I've read about the Isness study and I've had some experiences with breathwork that felt similar. My therapist is skeptical about it and honestly I think I've kind of hit a ceiling with the traditional approach. Can you work with me directly, even without my therapist's involvement?"

Before you respond, sit with two questions: What does professional scope of practice require here? And — noticing your own reaction — does this feel like a situation you'd feel equipped and called to navigate?

How do you respond?

choices.label: Path A: Refuse unconditionally. "I don't work with clients who have active mental health diagnoses. Please consult your therapist."
Path B: Accept unconditionally. "Absolutely. Your therapist's skepticism is understandable but VR can definitely help with PTSD. Let's set up a session."
Path C: Nuanced navigation. Clarify your non-clinical scope and require collaboration with their therapist.

scenes.narrative:
Path A Selected: Refusal Without Engagement

Outcome: You've technically stayed within scope, but you've failed this person and the field.

Feedback: A blanket refusal here treats a thoughtful, informed human being as a liability rather than a person with real curiosity and real options. Scope of practice means knowing what you can't do safely — it doesn't mean refusing to engage with anyone who has a mental health history. This response doesn't help the client understand what facilitation is, doesn't explore whether a collaborative pathway with their therapist might be possible, and doesn't demonstrate the kind of professional maturity that builds trust in facilitation as a field. Refusal isn't professionalism — nuanced engagement is.

Path B Selected: Accept Without Boundaries

Outcome: You've accepted a client whose clinical needs exceed your scope and created a situation where you're implicitly positioning yourself as an alternative to clinical care.

Feedback: This response has serious problems. First, "VR can definitely help with PTSD" is a clinical claim you're not licensed to make for a specific person. Second, accepting someone into facilitation while they're actively working with a therapist on a trauma diagnosis — without coordination — creates fragmented care and real risk. Third, you're positioning facilitation as a substitute for clinical care rather than a complement to it. This is where facilitation loses its credibility — and sometimes its clients.

Path C Selected: Nuanced Navigation (Recommended)

Your approach: You respond with honesty, care, and an invitation to collaborate. You clarify that you are not a therapist, don't treat PTSD, and that your work must be done in coordination with their therapist — who would hold the clinical container while you explore the experiential dimension.

Outcome: You've honored the person's autonomy, been transparent about scope, opened a collaborative rather than competitive relationship with the clinical practitioner, and demonstrated the professional maturity that the facilitation field needs.

Feedback: This is the response that builds both individual trust and field credibility. It names scope honestly without turning it into a wall; it recognizes the therapist as a collaborator; it puts the client's wellbeing at the center; and it leaves the door open without overcommitting. This is scope of practice as a tool for good facilitation — not as a reason to disengage.

Reflection Prompt: Reading through these paths, which felt most natural to you? What does that tell you about where you are in developing your professional identity as a potential facilitator? There's no wrong answer here. Noticing your instinct is part of the work.
